text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.

text: It reads with conscious understanding, then writes the whole answer at once.
feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
represented_misconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
distractor_rationale: Tempting because fluent text can sound like a human explanation.

text: It searches a hidden database and copies the best matching passage.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Search can be added around a model, but a plain LLM generates from context and learned weights.
represented_misconception: search engine/database vs generative model
distractor_rationale: Tempting because broad model knowledge can feel like search.

text: It changes its weights each time the user sends a new prompt.
feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.
represented_misconception: training vs inference
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model may seem to adapt within a conversation.

text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.

text: It must have inner awareness because the explanation sounds human.
feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
represented_misconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
distractor_rationale: Tempting because human-like language invites human-like interpretation.

text: It must have found a hidden perfect source and copied the interpretation.
feedback: Close, but this describes search or retrieval better than a plain LLM response. A fluent answer is not the same as cited evidence.
represented_misconception: database lookup vs learned generation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because polished answers can feel copied from a source.

text: The poem prompt permanently taught the base model a new skill.
feedback: That would require training. A normal prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.
represented_misconception: prompt/context change vs durable weight update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because earlier context can shape later replies in a chat.

text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and denoising image models are different branches.
feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.

text: All generative AI tools are LLMs because ChatGPT is generative AI.
feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: LLM vs generative AI family
distractor_rationale: Tempting because ChatGPT is many learners’ first generative AI example.

text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates language tokens.
feedback: Close, but image models can be AI without using the LLM text-token loop. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: LLM output vs broader generative outputs
distractor_rationale: Tempting because this app starts with language models.

text: AI and machine learning are the same label, so the branches do not matter.
feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI field.
represented_misconception: AI vs machine learning category boundary
distractor_rationale: Tempting because public language often blurs these words.

text: AI is broad; ML learns from data; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs generate language or code.
feedback: Insight strengthened. The map matters because an LLM is one branch inside a wider AI landscape.

text: All AI systems are LLMs if they use language.
feedback: That collapses the broad AI family into one model type. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: All AI systems are LLMs if they use language.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because LLM is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Machine learning and AI are exact synonyms.
feedback: That misses that machine learning is a major branch inside broader AI. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: Machine learning and AI are exact synonyms.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because machine learning is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same generation loop.
feedback: That treats different generative methods as if they shared one mechanism. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same generation loop.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because diffusion model is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.

text: The whole app, including every tool and button.
feedback: Not quite. The product can include an LLM, but the LLM is not every surrounding tool and screen.
represented_misconception: model vs product/app wrapper
distractor_rationale: Tempting because users experience the whole product as one AI thing.

text: The safety policy that blocks some outputs.
feedback: Close, but a safety policy shapes behavior around the model; it is not the language model itself.
represented_misconception: policy/guardrail vs model
distractor_rationale: Tempting because safety behavior is visible in the product.

text: The database or search index the app may call.
feedback: This describes a retrieval or search component. The LLM generates language from context and learned weights.
represented_misconception: retrieval/search component vs language model
distractor_rationale: Tempting because some chatbot products include search.

text: The rule-based system follows written rules; the LLM uses weights shaped by examples.
feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.

text: The rule-based system measures loss and updates weights after every task.
feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.
represented_misconception: symbolic rules vs training loop
distractor_rationale: Tempting because loss is nearby in the deep-learning explanation.

text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
feedback: That gives the LLM too much mind. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.
represented_misconception: conscious reasoning vs learned behavior
distractor_rationale: Tempting because LLM language can sound like reasoning from the inside.

text: There is no difference; both systems are just written rulebooks.
feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.
represented_misconception: LLM as hand-coded rulebook
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both systems can produce predictable outputs.

text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.

text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.
represented_misconception: loss vs human value judgment
distractor_rationale: Tempting because loss sounds like a judgment word.

text: Loss is the model’s confidence score for the next token during generation.
feedback: Close, but confidence or probability shapes generation. Loss measures training error so weights can be adjusted.
represented_misconception: loss vs confidence/probability during generation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both are numerical signals related to prediction.

text: Loss writes the final answer after the model has finished thinking.
feedback: Not quite. Loss is used during training; it is not the model writing a response. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: training signal vs response generation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because learners may treat every internal step as answer writing.

text: The product combines learned patterns, written rules, and lookup tools; the LLM remains learned-pattern AI.
feedback: Good distinction. Modern AI products can mix traditions without making the model itself rule-based.

text: The hand-written checks turn the LLM into symbolic AI.
feedback: That confuses a product wrapper with the model inside it. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: The hand-written checks turn the LLM into symbolic AI.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because symbolic AI is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The handbook lookup permanently rewrites the model weights.
feedback: Retrieval can add context without becoming durable training. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: The retrieved handbook permanently rewrites the model weights.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because RAG is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The policy checks prove the model has moral understanding.
feedback: Policy behavior is not the same as conscience or awareness. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: The policy checks prove the model has moral understanding.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because alignment is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.

text: The model appends the mistaken token to a normal live response.
feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.
represented_misconception: training vs autoregressive inference
distractor_rationale: Tempting because generation also chooses and appends tokens.

text: The prompt becomes durable memory stored inside the model’s weights.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
represented_misconception: context vs durable weight update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because chat context can feel remembered.

text: The model treats the most likely answer as automatically true.
feedback: Not quite. Likelihood is not truth, and this training moment matters because loss can drive a weight update.
represented_misconception: likelihood vs truth
distractor_rationale: Tempting because likely answers can sound authoritative.

text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite weights.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.

text: The chat permanently updates the base model’s weights.
feedback: Not quite. That would require a training process, not ordinary inference. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: ordinary inference vs training update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model appears to adapt to the user.

text: The model stores the prompt as durable memory inside its weights.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
represented_misconception: context vs durable memory/weights
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model can refer to earlier chat context.

text: The optimizer trains the model after every response.
feedback: Close, but optimizers update weights during training, not after every normal response.
represented_misconception: optimizer/weight update vs normal response
distractor_rationale: Tempting because training and inference both involve model computation.

text: Training changes reusable weights; inference uses temporary computation for the current run.
feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.

text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights for future runs.
feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.
represented_misconception: optimizer/weight update vs inference
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both training and inference run model computations.

text: Training only changes the visible prompt text for one response.
feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes learned numbers.
represented_misconception: prompt/context vs weights
distractor_rationale: Tempting because prompt text clearly changes a response.

text: Training guarantees that every future answer will be true.
feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.
represented_misconception: training improvement vs truth guarantee
distractor_rationale: Tempting because training sounds like making the model better.

text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.

text: Prompt -> response token -> append -> next response token.
feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: training loop vs autoregressive generation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because this is the model’s generation loop.

text: Search -> outside passage -> current context -> answer.
feedback: That describes a search or retrieval step. It can add temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.
represented_misconception: training vs retrieval/context
distractor_rationale: Tempting because outside text can improve an answer.

text: User correction -> better answer -> permanent learning inside the same chat.
feedback: Not quite. A correction can steer the current conversation, but durable learning requires a training process.
represented_misconception: conversation steering vs durable training
distractor_rationale: Tempting because a correction can improve the current conversation.

text: Repeated prediction loss on next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.

text: A user conversation at deployment permanently rewrites the model.
feedback: Not quite. Pretraining happens before normal use; an ordinary chat usually does not rewrite weights.
represented_misconception: pretraining vs inference
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model can seem to adapt inside a chat.

text: A human writes every rule the model will follow.
feedback: Close, but pretraining is learned from examples, not a complete rulebook written by hand.
represented_misconception: learned patterns vs symbolic rules
distractor_rationale: Tempting because software often has hand-written rules.

text: The model stores a perfect copy of every source document.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Pretraining shapes weights; it is not perfect document storage.
represented_misconception: pattern learning vs perfect source recall
distractor_rationale: Tempting because source data influences the model.

text: It shapes patterns in weights; it is not a searchable copy of every source.
feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining can shape broad ability without turning the model into a perfect source library.

text: It gives the model direct access to every private file on demand.
feedback: Not quite. Training data exposure is not the same as access to every private file. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: training data vs universal file access
distractor_rationale: Tempting because broad training data can sound like broad access.

text: It inserts the original source into every future prompt context.
feedback: Close, but adding source text to context is different from pretraining. Pretraining changes weights before use.
represented_misconception: pretraining vs retrieval/context
distractor_rationale: Tempting because source material can sometimes be added to a prompt.

text: It happens only after a user asks a specific question.
feedback: Pretraining happens before deployment. The user prompt later uses the pretrained weights.
represented_misconception: pretraining vs deployment-time inference
distractor_rationale: Tempting because learners meet the model only during use.

text: Weights shift so future prompts can activate useful language or code patterns.
feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining changes reusable model structure before later prompts arrive.

text: The model’s current chat context becomes permanently larger.
feedback: Not quite. Context is temporary input; pretraining changes reusable weights before later prompts arrive.
represented_misconception: pretraining weights vs temporary context
distractor_rationale: Tempting because context also affects output.

text: Only the app’s buttons, labels, or interface change.
feedback: This describes product UI changes, not the model’s learned parameters. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: model weights vs product interface
distractor_rationale: Tempting because users meet models through apps.

text: Only a safety policy is added around the model.
feedback: Close, but a policy can shape a product around the model; pretraining changes the model’s reusable weights.
represented_misconception: pretraining vs product policy wrapper
distractor_rationale: Tempting because products often include safety behavior.

text: Past training shaped weights; source-like text is not proof of live search.
feedback: Good boundary. Pretraining can shape fact-like patterns without turning the model into a search engine.

text: The model must have used a live lookup tool during the prompt.
feedback: Search is a system/tool action, not something pretraining proves. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The model must have searched the web during the prompt.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because retrieval is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The model opened a stored copy of the original page.
feedback: Pretraining does not make a perfect searchable library of every source. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The model opened a stored copy of the original page.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The model became certain because the text sounded factual.
feedback: Fluent source-like text is not a truth guarantee. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The model became certain because the text sounded factual.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because truth is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly to generalize well.
feedback: Good diagnosis. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.

text: Generalization: the model is working well on unseen cases.
feedback: Not quite. Generalization means good performance on new examples; this scenario shows the opposite.
represented_misconception: overfitting vs generalization
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both terms describe training performance.

text: Better training: the model has clearly learned the general rule.
feedback: Not quite. If it fails on new validation examples, high training performance is not enough evidence of generalization.
represented_misconception: training performance vs validation failure
distractor_rationale: Tempting because perfect training performance feels reassuring.

text: Inference: the model is simply generating one token at a time.
feedback: Close, but inference is normal model use; overfitting is a training/evaluation failure mode.
represented_misconception: training/evaluation failure vs inference process
distractor_rationale: Tempting because all later model use involves inference.

text: They are kept out of training so they can test whether learned patterns transfer.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.

text: Validation data is just extra training data used to lower loss.
feedback: Not quite. Validation examples are saved for testing, not used as extra examples to fit the model.
represented_misconception: validation data vs training data
distractor_rationale: Tempting because validation examples can look like more examples.

text: Training examples alone prove the model will work everywhere.
feedback: This choice reveals the exact overfitting risk. Seen examples are not enough evidence.
represented_misconception: training performance vs generalization
distractor_rationale: Tempting because high training accuracy feels reassuring.

text: A lower loss always proves every future answer is true.
feedback: Close, but lower loss is not a truth guarantee, especially outside the tested examples.
represented_misconception: loss/probability vs truth guarantee
distractor_rationale: Tempting because lower loss often signals improvement.

text: It may have fit the narrow examples too closely instead of learning a pattern that generalizes.
feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but narrow training can also make behavior brittle.

text: The prompts permanently rewrote the model during inference.
feedback: Not quite. The issue is training behavior, not prompts rewriting weights during inference.
represented_misconception: prompt/context vs training weight changes
distractor_rationale: Tempting because prompts can change output behavior.

text: The model learned the narrow template so well that broader prompts no longer matter.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The issue is brittle transfer, not intention. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: brittle transfer vs intention/conscious choice
distractor_rationale: Tempting because poor behavior can seem intentional.

text: Rule-based AI is always better than learned models.
feedback: This overgeneralizes. The issue is narrow training behavior, not proof that rules always beat learned models.
represented_misconception: overfitting diagnosis vs symbolic superiority claim
distractor_rationale: Tempting because brittleness can make rules feel safer.

text: Run additional training that changes weights or adapter behavior for later responses.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Fine-tuning is durable adaptation for future inference runs.

text: Paste the norms into one user prompt.
feedback: A prompt can steer the current run, but it is not durable training. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Paste the norms into one user prompt.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because prompt is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Retrieve a policy page into the current context.
feedback: Retrieval adds temporary evidence; it does not by itself fine-tune weights. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Retrieve a policy page into the current context.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because RAG is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Sample from a lower-temperature distribution.
feedback: Sampling changes a generation-time choice, not the model pattern carried forward. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Sample from a lower-temperature distribution.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because sampling is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Adapted weights or adapter behavior can carry the pattern into later inference runs.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Fine-tuning makes a reusable model change, not just a one-chat hint.

text: Only the current context window got larger.
feedback: Not quite. A larger context window is temporary input space; fine-tuning changes reusable behavior.
represented_misconception: context window vs fine-tuned behavior
distractor_rationale: Tempting because context can also carry institution-specific text.

text: The model became conscious of the institution’s norms.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The behavior can be shaped without conscious commitment to norms.
represented_misconception: mind/metaphor overreach
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the assistant may sound norm-aware.

text: The model must search the institution’s files every time.
feedback: Close, but fine-tuning and retrieval are different. Retrieval may be added, but fine-tuned behavior can persist in weights or adapters.
represented_misconception: retrieval vs fine-tuning
distractor_rationale: Tempting because many institutional assistants also use search.

text: Training can make instruction-following patterns more likely later.
feedback: Good distinction. Fine-tuning can support alignment by durably shaping output patterns.

text: Future answers will tend to be safer, so evaluation is no longer needed.
feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth or safety.
represented_misconception: alignment/fine-tuning as guarantee
distractor_rationale: Tempting because alignment sounds like solving behavior.

text: Only the visible system prompt changed for one session.
feedback: Close, but a system prompt steers the current run. Fine-tuning is durable training. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: runtime steering vs durable training
distractor_rationale: Tempting because system prompts can steer behavior strongly.

text: The model acquired human values and moral agency.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Fine-tuning changes output patterns; it does not create moral agency.
represented_misconception: behavior shaping vs moral agency
distractor_rationale: Tempting because aligned behavior can sound value-driven.

text: Behavior was shaped toward instructions and safety boundaries, not moral understanding.
feedback: Good distinction. Alignment shapes behavior; it is not magic morality.

text: The model must understand morality the way a person does.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Refusal behavior can be shaped without human moral understanding.
represented_misconception: aligned behavior vs moral agency
distractor_rationale: Tempting because refusals can sound principled.

text: Safer wording means the factual claims have already been verified.
feedback: Not quite. Alignment can shape behavior, but it does not guarantee factual truth. Probability, permission, fluency, or confidence is not the same as truth.
represented_misconception: alignment vs truth guarantee
distractor_rationale: Tempting because safer behavior can feel more trustworthy.

text: The LLM stopped using learned weights and became a rulebook.
feedback: Close, but an aligned product can still use a learned model plus rules, filters, or policies.
represented_misconception: alignment controls vs learned model
distractor_rationale: Tempting because aligned products may use rules or filters.

text: Some alignment changes are durable training; others steer or filter the current run.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Alignment can happen through several layers, not one magic switch.

text: Every alignment layer permanently rewrites the model weights.
feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning may change weights, but system prompts and filters can steer behavior at runtime.
represented_misconception: runtime control vs durable weight update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because all layers affect visible behavior.

text: Alignment is only a better prompt typed by the user.
feedback: Close, but alignment can include training, policies, evaluation, and runtime controls beyond the user prompt.
represented_misconception: alignment vs user prompting only
distractor_rationale: Tempting because prompts are visible and powerful.

text: Alignment is the same thing as sampling the most probable token.
feedback: Sampling chooses a next token from probabilities. Alignment shapes or constrains behavior around that process.
represented_misconception: alignment vs decoding
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both affect the final response.

text: Because shaped behavior can still fail, drift, or create unexpected tradeoffs in new situations.
feedback: Good distinction. Alignment needs evaluation; it is not a one-time guarantee.

text: To check whether the model has developed a conscience.
feedback: Not quite. Evaluation checks behavior and outcomes, not inner conscience. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: evaluation vs consciousness
distractor_rationale: Tempting because evaluations judge behavior.

text: Because aligned models no longer need human review.
feedback: This choice overclaims. Aligned systems can still need review, monitoring, and accountability.
represented_misconception: alignment vs human review
distractor_rationale: Tempting because alignment sounds like completion.

text: Because lower training loss alone proves the model is safe.
feedback: Close, but loss alone is not a full safety or usefulness evaluation. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: loss vs safety/behavior evaluation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because loss is an important training signal.

text: A policy or filter layer changed visible output, not necessarily model weights.
feedback: Good distinction. Product-level controls can affect what users see without being a weight update.

text: The model learned new moral values during that one response.
feedback: A blocked output is not evidence of a new conscience or durable training. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: The model learned new moral values during that one response.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because alignment is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The user prompt permanently rewrote the safety behavior.
feedback: Prompting can steer a run, but it usually does not update weights. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: The user prompt permanently rewrote the safety behavior.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because prompt is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: A filter approval means the answer no longer needs evidence review.
feedback: A safety decision is not the same as fact verification. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The answer became guaranteed true because it was allowed through.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because truth is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Temporary internal states change; learned weights usually stay fixed.
feedback: Good distinction. Inference uses the map without redrawing it.

text: The learned weights change after every user prompt.
feedback: Not quite. Ordinary inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.
represented_misconception: inference vs training update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model may adapt within a conversation.

text: The training dataset changes to include the new prompt.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A prompt can be current context without joining the training dataset.
represented_misconception: prompt/context vs training data
distractor_rationale: Tempting because prompts feel like new information.

text: The model’s conscious attention shifts to the user’s intent.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Inference computes temporary vectors; it is not conscious attention.
represented_misconception: attention/activation vs awareness
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the response may seem attentive.

text: Context enters a forward pass, temporary states form, raw scores appear, and a token can be chosen.
feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the live next-token path through inference.

text: Example enters training, loss updates weights, and a future model checkpoint is saved.
feedback: Close, but that is a training path. Inference uses existing weights to produce the next-token scores.
represented_misconception: training loop vs inference trace
distractor_rationale: Tempting because it is also a model computation path.

text: Retriever searches outside documents, then the model permanently learns the snippets.
feedback: Retrieval can add context before inference, but it does not by itself make the model permanently learn snippets.
represented_misconception: retrieval/context vs durable learning
distractor_rationale: Tempting because retrieval may happen before generation.

text: Tokenizer splits text and the answer appears without model layers.
feedback: Tokenization prepares input, but inference still runs model layers to produce scores. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: tokenization vs model forward pass
distractor_rationale: Tempting because tokenization is an early required step.

text: The earlier line is still in context, so fixed weights can use it during inference.
feedback: Good distinction. Context can feel like memory while still being temporary input.

text: The earlier line was written into the model’s weights.
feedback: Not quite. Same-conversation context is temporary input, not necessarily a weight update.
represented_misconception: context vs durable memory
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model appears to remember.

text: The chatbot fine-tuned itself on the earlier line.
feedback: Close, but adaptation within a context window is not the same as fine-tuning. Prompt and context can steer a current run without automatically becoming durable learning.
represented_misconception: ordinary inference vs fine-tuning
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the answer adapts to the conversation.

text: The model searched every document it was pretrained on.
feedback: This describes search better than ordinary inference. The model can use visible context without searching training sources.
represented_misconception: inference vs search/retrieval
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the answer may include earlier information.

text: A useful answer now does not automatically teach the base model for tomorrow.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Inference can be useful without being durable training.

text: Every good answer automatically becomes permanent skill.
feedback: Not quite. Durable skill changes require training or another model-update process. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: inference vs durable learning
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the response looks like successful practice.

text: The current prompt cannot affect the answer at all.
feedback: Close, but fixed weights can still respond differently to different current contexts. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: context influence vs weight update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because weights stay fixed.

text: There is no need to review outputs because inference is temporary.
feedback: This choice overclaims. Temporary outputs can still affect people and decisions, so review can matter.
represented_misconception: temporary computation vs output risk
distractor_rationale: Tempting because temporary sounds harmless.

text: Prompt tokens are given; response tokens are generated and appended to context.
feedback: Good distinction. Prompt is given; response is generated and appended.

text: Both prompt and response tokens were typed by the user.
feedback: Not quite. The user provides the prompt; the model generates response tokens. Prompt and context can steer a current run without automatically becoming durable learning.
represented_misconception: prompt vs generated response
distractor_rationale: Tempting because both can appear together in the conversation transcript.

text: The model writes the whole response at once after reading the prompt.
feedback: Close, but the model generates response tokens step by step. Prompt and context can steer a current run without automatically becoming durable learning.
represented_misconception: autoregressive generation vs full answer at once
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the interface may show a smooth answer.

text: Each response token permanently updates the model’s weights.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Response tokens can shape the current run without updating weights.
represented_misconception: generated context vs durable training
distractor_rationale: Tempting because response-so-far shapes later tokens.

text: The original prompt plus the response so far, including the new token.
feedback: Insight strengthened. The context grows as generated response tokens are appended.

text: Only the newly chosen token, with the prompt removed.
feedback: Not quite. The next step can use the prompt and response-so-far that remain in context.
represented_misconception: context accumulation vs isolated token
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the new token is the most recent event.

text: A permanent memory of the token stored in model weights.
feedback: Close, but using a token later in the run is context, not a weight update. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: response-so-far vs weight memory
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model uses the new token later.

text: No context, because softmax disappears after one token.
feedback: Softmax can help choose a token, but the chosen token can still be appended to the next context.
represented_misconception: decoding step vs context persistence
distractor_rationale: Tempting because softmax is a later token-choice mechanism.

text: Better current context helped the next response tokens come from better input.
feedback: Good distinction. Better context can improve the current response without training the model.

text: The model was fine-tuned by the clarifying sentence.
feedback: Not quite. A clarifying sentence can steer the current run; fine-tuning is additional training.
represented_misconception: prompt steering vs fine-tuning
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the answer improves after feedback.

text: The model understood its mistake like a person and decided to improve.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Better output can come from better context. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: human revision vs generated behavior
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the interaction resembles human revision.

text: Prompts cannot affect generated response tokens.
feedback: Close, but fixed weights still process different prompts into different response probabilities.
represented_misconception: prompt influence vs fixed weights
distractor_rationale: Tempting because weights stay fixed during inference.

text: Tokens can be uneven text chunks, including word pieces and punctuation.
feedback: Good distinction. Tokens are model-readable chunks, not always whole words.

text: Every token must be a complete dictionary word.
feedback: Not quite. Tokenizers can split words, punctuation, spaces, or other chunks. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: token vs word
distractor_rationale: Tempting because many displayed tokens look word-like.

text: Each token is a permanent memory stored in the model.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A token is a representation chunk, not memory by itself.
represented_misconception: token vs memory
distractor_rationale: Tempting because tokens are reused in later processing.

text: Each token is exactly one human concept.
feedback: Close, but token boundaries do not perfectly match human concepts or meanings. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: token chunk vs meaning/concept
distractor_rationale: Tempting because tokens often carry meaning clues.

text: Text is split into tokens, tokens map to token IDs, and IDs look up embeddings.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Tokenization starts the bridge from text to numerical computation.

text: Raw English sentences move through every layer without numerical representation.
feedback: Not quite. The model needs numerical representations, starting with tokens and IDs. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: raw text vs numerical representation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because users see text at the interface.

text: Weights are rewritten before text becomes tokens.
feedback: Close, but tokenization prepares the input; it does not rewrite weights. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: tokenization vs training update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because weights are central to the model.

text: The model generates the finished response before tokenization happens.
feedback: Generation also uses tokens. Text needs token representation before model layers can process it.
represented_misconception: generation order vs tokenization
distractor_rationale: Tempting because users notice the final text first.

text: Prompt text and generated response text can both be represented as tokens.
feedback: Good distinction. Tokenization applies to text entering and leaving the generation loop.

text: Only prompt text becomes tokens; response text skips token IDs.
feedback: Not quite. Generated response tokens are token IDs before being displayed as text. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: prompt token vs response token
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the user sees the response as text.

text: Only response text becomes tokens; prompts stay as raw English.
feedback: Close, but prompts also need token representation before model processing. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: prompt token vs raw prompt
distractor_rationale: Tempting because prompts feel natural to humans.

text: Tokenizing either one permanently trains the model.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Tokenization represents text; it does not update weights.
represented_misconception: tokenization vs training
distractor_rationale: Tempting because tokenization is a required model step.

text: To look up a learned starting vector in the embedding table.
feedback: Good distinction. The ID is a lookup key for an embedding vector.

text: To store the complete meaning of dog inside the number itself.
feedback: Not quite. The number itself is not the meaning; it points to learned numerical patterns.
represented_misconception: ID number vs learned meaning pattern
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the ID consistently points to that token.

text: To permanently remember this user’s sentence.
feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Token IDs represent chunks; they do not store a user memory.
represented_misconception: representation vs durable memory
distractor_rationale: Tempting because IDs are reusable in model processing.

text: To skip embeddings and tensors entirely.
feedback: Close, but the ID is a lookup key; embeddings and tensors still carry numerical representation forward.
represented_misconception: token ID vs embedding/tensor pipeline
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the ID is already numeric.

text: 982 is a lookup number; meaning comes from embeddings, layers, and context.
feedback: Insight strengthened. The ID points into the system; it is not understanding by itself.

text: 982 means cat because the model consciously understands the number.
feedback: That gives the model too much mind. A lookup number does not imply conscious understanding.
represented_misconception: ID/meaning vs consciousness
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the model uses the ID fluently.

text: 982 guarantees every sentence about cats will be true.
feedback: Not quite. Token IDs do not guarantee factual output. Probability, permission, fluency, or confidence is not the same as truth.
represented_misconception: token ID vs truth
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the token has a stable identity.

text: 982 is randomly redefined every time the user sends a prompt.
feedback: Close, but token ID mappings are fixed for a tokenizer/model setup; sampling randomness is a different step.
represented_misconception: stable tokenizer mapping vs per-run randomness
distractor_rationale: Tempting because sampling can be random later.

text: The chosen ID can display as text and append as a response token.
feedback: Good distinction. Generated tokens are IDs before they become visible text.

text: The chosen ID permanently updates the model weights.
feedback: Not quite. The chosen token can affect the next context without changing weights. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: generated token vs training update
distractor_rationale: Tempting because the token affects the next step.

text: The model skips IDs and chooses only raw human words.
feedback: Close, but the model works with token IDs before text is displayed. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: token IDs vs raw text generation
distractor_rationale: Tempting because users see words on screen.

text: The ID retrieves the original training document for that word.
feedback: This describes retrieval better than token ID lookup. Token IDs point to token representations, not original documents.
represented_misconception: token ID lookup vs retrieval/source
distractor_rationale: Tempting because lookup sounds like fetching a source.

text: A learned starting vector from the embedding table.
feedback: Insight strengthened. The embedding is the token ID's learned numerical starting point.

text: The temporary hidden state after attention has read the sentence.
feedback: That describes a later context-shaped vector, not the starting lookup. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The temporary hidden state after attention has read the sentence.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because hidden state is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The integer ID itself, before any lookup happens.
feedback: The ID is a lookup key, not the vector returned by the table. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The integer ID itself, before any lookup happens.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token ID is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: A human-readable dictionary definition stored in English.
feedback: Embeddings are numerical vectors, not text definitions. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: A human-readable dictionary definition stored in English.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because definition is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Context and layers reshape the starting vector into a temporary internal state.
feedback: Good distinction. Embeddings start the token; hidden states are shaped by context during inference.

text: The embedding table rewrites itself during ordinary inference.
feedback: Ordinary inference usually uses fixed learned weights. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: The embedding table rewrites itself during ordinary inference.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because weight update is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The token ID changes meaning by becoming a new integer.
feedback: The ID remains a lookup key; later vectors carry contextual information. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: The token ID changes meaning by becoming a new integer.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token ID is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: The model stores a permanent memory of each sentence.
feedback: Temporary hidden states are not permanent memories. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: The model stores a permanent memory of each sentence.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is an ordered numerical representation the model can compute with.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Vectors let the model carry many numerical features at once.

text: It is a single integer lookup key.
feedback: That describes a token ID, not a vector of many values. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: It is a single integer lookup key.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token ID is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is a shaped block with several axes such as tokens and features.
feedback: That is closer to a tensor; a vector is one-dimensional. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: It is a shaped block with several axes such as tokens and features.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because tensor is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is the visible word shown to the user.
feedback: Visible text is converted before the model computes with vectors. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: It is the visible word shown to the user.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Features are distributed across dimensions, not one neat human label each.
feedback: Good distinction. Vector meaning is spread out and computed, not stored as neat labels.

text: Each dimension is a named English category.
feedback: That makes distributed features too literal. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: Each dimension is a named English category.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because feature is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Each vector is a saved chat memory.
feedback: A vector representation is not a stored conversation. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Each vector is a saved chat memory.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Each vector is a complete citation to a source.
feedback: Vectors do not carry source citations by themselves. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: Each vector is a complete citation to a source.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because citation is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: One can be a durable learned parameter; the other is temporary during a run.
feedback: Good boundary. Some vectors live in learned model data, while others are temporary activations.

text: Both are permanent memories of the user conversation.
feedback: Conversation memory is a different product feature, not what these vectors are. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Both are permanent memories of the user conversation.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Both are token IDs before embedding lookup.
feedback: A token ID is a scalar lookup key, not the vector itself. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: Both are token IDs before embedding lookup.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token ID is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Both are visible words after generation.
feedback: Visible text is the display side, not the internal vector state. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Both are visible words after generation.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because response token is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is a shaped numerical block with dimensions model operations can use.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Tensors organize numbers so layers can process positions, features, batches, or heads.

text: It is one token ID for one word piece.
feedback: A token ID is a lookup key, not a shaped numerical block. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: It is one token ID for one word piece.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token ID is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is a rule written by a programmer.
feedback: A tensor is data the model computes over, not an if-then rule. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: It is a rule written by a programmer.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because rule-based AI is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: It is a permanent memory of every prompt.
feedback: Tensors can be temporary activations or learned parameters, not conversation memory by default.
represented_misconception: It is a permanent memory of every prompt.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Weight tensors can be durable; activation tensors are temporary run-time values.
feedback: Good distinction. Tensor names can describe shape, while durability depends on whether the tensor is a parameter or an activation.

text: All tensors are temporary and vanish after every token.
feedback: That ignores learned parameter tensors. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: All tensors are temporary and vanish after every token.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because activation is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: All tensors are permanent and become model memory.
feedback: That ignores temporary activation tensors. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: All tensors are permanent and become model memory.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Tensors are only visible words arranged in rows.
feedback: The model uses numerical arrays, not visible text tables. The tempting idea is nearby, but it does not match the boundary this checkpoint is testing.
represented_misconception: Tensors are only visible words arranged in rows.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because token is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Weighting relevance between token positions in the current context.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Attention is weighted relevance between positions, not awareness.

text: Giving the model conscious focus on the sentence.
feedback: Attention here is a calculation, not human attention. The behavior can be shaped without giving the model human awareness, intent, or moral agency.
represented_misconception: Giving the model conscious focus on the sentence.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because awareness is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Choosing the final next token from probabilities.
feedback: Sampling happens later after scores become probabilities. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Choosing the final next token from probabilities.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because sampling is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Rewriting the model weights to remember the sentence.
feedback: The attention pattern is temporary during inference. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Rewriting the model weights to remember the sentence.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because weight update is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Learned attention weights persist, but one prompt's attention pattern is temporary.
feedback: Good boundary. The current relevance pattern is made for this run and does not become stored memory by itself.

text: Every attention link becomes a saved user memory.
feedback: Temporary relevance patterns are not saved memory. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Every attention link becomes a saved user memory.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Attention is the same as a retrieval system searching documents.
feedback: Retrieval can add text to context; attention works inside the model over positions. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Attention is the same as a retrieval system searching documents.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because retrieval is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Attention replaces the need for hidden states.
feedback: Attention helps shape hidden states rather than replacing them. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Attention replaces the need for hidden states.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because hidden state is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Attention mixes information across positions; the MLP reshapes features within each position.
feedback: Good distinction. The two operations help in different ways inside a layer.

text: Attention reshapes features within one position while the MLP searches documents.
feedback: That swaps the roles and adds retrieval where it does not belong. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Attention reshapes features within one position while the MLP searches documents.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because MLP is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Attention chooses the next token while the MLP converts logits to probabilities.
feedback: Next-token choice and softmax happen later in the Decision Room. That swaps this mechanism with a nearby model step; keep the stage boundary clear.
represented_misconception: Attention chooses the next token while the MLP converts logits to probabilities.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because sampling is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Attention is human awareness while the MLP is moral judgment.

represented_misconception: Attention is human awareness while the MLP is moral judgment.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because awareness is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Inside transformer layers, shaping temporary hidden states from current context.
feedback: Insight strengthened. Attention is one layer operation in the forward pass.

text: Before tokenization, deciding how text should be split.

represented_misconception: Before tokenization, deciding how text should be split.

text: Outside the model, searching every connected file.

represented_misconception: Outside the model, searching every connected file.

text: The MLP, using learned weights to transform features at each token position.

text: Attention, because attention is the per-token feature reshaper.

distractor_rationale: Plausible because grounding is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Weights are durable; context/states are temporary; retrieval adds context; responses are generated.
feedback: Good boundary. Keeping these buckets separate prevents many myths.

text: Everything useful becomes permanent memory after one answer.
feedback: Usefulness during a run does not imply durable storage. That would be a durable learning or storage claim; this checkpoint is separating it from temporary context or runtime behavior.
represented_misconception: Everything useful becomes permanent memory after one answer.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because memory is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Retrieved evidence is the same as fine-tuning.
feedback: Retrieval supplies context; fine-tuning changes learned behavior. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: Retrieved evidence is the same as fine-tuning.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because RAG is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Generated response tokens prove the answer is true.
feedback: Generated text still needs evidence and review. Search, retrieval, and evidence use are separate system steps, and they still need checking.
represented_misconception: Generated response tokens prove the answer is true.
distractor_rationale: Plausible because truth is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.

text: Look for grounding, sources, uncertainty, and human review instead of treating fluency as wisdom.

text: Assume fluent output equals understanding.

distractor_rationale: Plausible because understanding is nearby, but this choice preserves a misconception the card is designed to correct.
